vocabulary.definition: The development of modules and module hierarchies.
The identification of the problems that a software needs to solve; the first step in Jackson System Development (JSD).
The point when a design becomes a working piece of software.
A method for ensuring that software fulfills real-life requirements.
The expression of a design as a set of communicating processes.
A chart that shows the functions of a system used by engineers to guide software designs.
A method of software development consisting of three distinct phases: modeling, network, and implementation.
The code used in structure diagrams to represent the logic of a system.
A diagram representing compound components in a structure, showing all functions and modules a completed structure will have.
The practice of mapping the movement of data through a system, often represented graphically.
A method of system development based on data flow and program structure.
A graphical representation of data flow used to optimize the functions of a system.
A characteristic that can be used to identify an object.
Refers to all of the fundamental qualities of an object at a given time.
The interaction between two entities.
A method used by software developers to design systems according to the objects they contain.
An unchanging quality of one entity.
A design strategy that organizes system architecture around objects rather than functions.
A software modeling language and process used for object-oriented analysis and design.
An image that depicts objects in relation to a particular interaction.
An image that measures the time ordering of events within an interaction.
An image that depicts sequences of messages sent between objects.
An image that models relationships between nodes and depicts the decomposition of a system.
An image that depicts the dynamic behavior of single objects.
A software development system that structures the process into three distinct phases.
A group of objects that share similar attributes.
Faults that are predicted by engineers during the testing process.
A mistake made by an engineer in a software's code or input.
A testing model that attempts to stop faults before they occur.
A model used to detect design and implementation faults.
A set of requirements that software must meet before it passes a test.
A program or tool used by engineers to compare test results with expected results.
An internal defect in software caused by human error.
An observable occurrence where software does not perform as required.
The practice of stopping problems from occurring in the first place.
The process of identifying existing problems in the software.
To meet certain requirements or criteria set by engineers.
A testing model that ensures software completes all required tasks.
A testing model specifically designed to detect implementation faults.
Testing conducted while a program is running.
A process that formally states a program's specifications to prove it works as intended.
Testing without looking at the internal code structure.
Testing that focuses primarily on identifying problems or faults.
Testing measured by the amount of code it actually exercises.
Testing done where the internal structure/code is known.
Testing that assesses common situations or use cases.
Testing looking specifically for common human mistakes.
A formal testing process involving a team of engineers.
Testing done on code that is not being executed.
A technique analyzing software starting with its most primitive parts.
A manual review of software done by a third-party engineer.
States that programs with the same structure and dataflow should still be tested on different criteria.
States that an adequate test set exists for every program.
States that a criterion should not require exhaustive testing.
States that programs differing only in unimportant ways can use the same test sets.
States that an empty set is not an adequate test set for any program.
States that components tested as part of a whole should still be tested alone.
States that every possible action/statement should be executed by its test sets.
States that software can always be tested further.
Rules used to determine if a software project has been tested sufficiently.
Programs with more components require more testing.
The need to test a whole program even if parts were tested in isolation.
Equivalence in function doesn't mean equivalence in required testing if structure differs.
The practice of accommodating new user requirements to improve software.
The practice of repairing software faults or bugs.
The format of a system with no clear order, making it difficult to maintain.
The practice of making systems easier to maintain in the future.
The process of fixing faults and making improvements in software after release.
A system should undergo modification until it is no longer cost-effective.
Updating software according to changes in the environment, such as a new OS.
Software structure becomes more complex with every change.
Attributes of a system that are unsuitable or not strong enough for updated environments.
To fix problems found in code.
A new updated version of existing software.
To improve existing components in new versions of software products.
The process of creating functionally equivalent versions of disorganized programs.
An old software system that has been updated multiple times but remains in use.
The process of creating documentation for programs that exist at the same level of abstraction.
A state where two programs serve the same purpose in the same way.
Software that is accessed via the internet rather than being disc-based.
Creating new versions of software at higher levels of abstraction by analyzing existing code.
To update old software with current technologies instead of creating new software from scratch.
Creating a new user interface for existing software.
Making substantial changes to a software system to improve it.
The process where engineers make functional changes to a software system.
Transforming systems from one representation to another without changing the functionality.
Concerns the dialog between systems and users, including the keystroke and syntax levels.
Concerns the functions the systems will perform for users, including the task and semantic levels.
Considers both the graphics of the user interface and the system hardware.
A model rendered in terms of a system's reactions to user actions.
Concerns the way a user understands a computer system.
Considers what a user needs to understand about a system in order to operate it.
Considers the interactions between a human and a system.
Describes the user interface of all aspects of a system.
Describes the attributes of a system that are relevant to the user.
A conceptual model that focuses on icons and screen layouts.
Software that assists groups in working towards a common goal.
All aspects of a system that are perceptible to a user.
The practice of using graphic design in user interfaces.
The consumer who will ultimately use a product.
A level of system operation.
A way of evaluating a complex system.
A model that splits user interface design into three distinct parts.
The study and design of interactions between computers and users.
The study of making physical components comfortable for users.
Interaction between users and computers.
Divides an application from its user interface.
The range of operations a system can perform.
Comprises a system's hardware and software as seen by the user.
Includes the way people think and react to various features.
Design focusing extensively on the experience of people using the software.
Software that is unmodified from its original state and available for public purchase.
A skill or specific method used to perform a task.
The extent or area that a software or project covers.
The components, concepts, and procedures of something.
Done in a particular instance only, rather than as a general rule.
Something that is available for purchase.
A list of commands to be executed in a computer program.
A situation created by a rising demand for new applications.
The practice of recycling pieces of code into new applications.
A way of dealing with something or a development methodology.
How something is utilized or applied.
Software reuse that requires engineers to modify the code.
Software reuse that does not require engineers to modify the software.
A reuse approach where existing components can easily be reused in new systems.
A reuse approach where software can be used to create new programs.
Provides a plan for the architecture of a new piece of software.
A product ready to be used in the development of new software.
The practice of finding and reusing code from existing sources.
A programming language that has a very high level of abstraction.
A process that identifies reusable code and information within a specific field of study.
Provides various support tools for developers to create software more efficiently.
Helps turn vague plans or high-level designs into detailed, functional systems.
Provides a plan for the overall structure of a software system, focusing on how modules connect.
To create a specific instance of an object or component.
An unfinished component provided by application generators for engineers to use as a starting point.
An unfinished program in the early stages of development that provides the basic structure.
A collection of available codes that allows engineers to search for usable pieces and insert them into new software.
Software that connects applications to operating systems and ensures they can run simultaneously without conflict.
